questions.question: A test for a rare disease has a sensitivity of 96% (probability of testing positive given disease) and specificity of 94% (probability of testing negative given no disease). If the disease occurs in 0.5% of the population, what is the probability that a person who tests positive actually has the disease? Round to the nearest percent.
questions.explanation: Use Bayes' theorem: P(disease|positive) = P(positive|disease) × P(disease) / P(positive). P(positive) = 0.96(0.005) + 0.06(0.995) = 0.0648. So P(disease|positive) = (0.96 × 0.005) / 0.0648 ≈ 0.074 or 7%.

questions.question: A two-way table shows students by grade and whether they participate in sports. Of 200 total students, 85 are in Grade 10 (rest are Grade 11). Among Grade 10 students, 60% play sports. Among Grade 11 students, 55% play sports. If a student is randomly selected and plays sports, what is the probability they are in Grade 11? Express as a fraction in lowest terms.
questions.explanation: Grade 10: 85 × 0.60 = 51 play sports. Grade 11: 115 × 0.55 = 63.25 ≈ 63 play sports (using whole students, 63 play). Total sports players ≈ 51 + 63 = 114. Wait: recalculate with exact arithmetic—115 × 0.55 = 63.25. Use 51 and 63.25: total = 114.25; P(G11|sports) = 63.25/114.25 ≈ 69/131.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Given that the sum is at least 10, what is the probability that both dice show the same number?
questions.explanation: Sums ≥ 10: (4,6), (5,5), (6,4), (5,6), (6,5), (6,6)—that's 6 outcomes. Same number: only (5,5) and (6,6)—that's 2 outcomes. P(same|sum≥10) = 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.question: A bag contains 4 red marbles, 5 blue marbles, and 3 green marbles. You draw two marbles without replacement. What is the probability that the marbles are different colors?
questions.explanation: Total ways to draw 2 from 12: C(12,2) = 66. Same color: C(4,2) + C(5,2) + C(3,2) = 6 + 10 + 3 = 19. Different colors: 66 − 19 = 47. Wait—recount: 47/66. Actually 70/132 = 35/66 is the reduced form; verify: P(different) = 1 − P(same) = 1 − 19/66 = 47/66.

questions.question: A company reports that 3% of its employees work remotely full-time and 12% work remotely part-time. Among full-time remote workers, 85% use company-issued laptops. Among part-time remote workers, 70% use company-issued laptops. If an employee uses a company laptop, what is the probability they work remotely (either full-time or part-time)?
questions.explanation: P(laptop) = 0.03(0.85) + 0.12(0.70) = 0.0255 + 0.084 = 0.1095. P(remote|laptop) = P(laptop and remote) / P(laptop) = 0.1095 / 0.1095... Actually compute: P(FT remote and laptop) + P(PT remote and laptop) = 0.0255 + 0.084 = 0.1095. This is numerator. For denominator, add non-remote workers: needs more info. Assume: P(remote|laptop) = (0.03 × 0.85 + 0.12 × 0.70) / (0.03 × 0.85 + 0.12 × 0.70 + P(non-remote and laptop)). If 85% of population is non-remote and 40% of them use laptops: total = 0.1095 + 0.85(0.40) = 0.1095 + 0.34 = 0.4495. P(remote|laptop) = 0.1095 / 0.4495 ≈ 0.244. Revised: answer is context-dependent; best answer is 0.348 if non-remote laptop usage ~20%.

questions.question: In a lottery, the probability of winning the jackpot is 1/1,000,000. The probability of winning any prize is 1/50. If you buy 100 tickets, what is the probability you win at least one prize (not necessarily the jackpot)?
questions.explanation: P(no prize in one ticket) = 49/50. P(no prize in 100 tickets) = (49/50)^100 ≈ 0.1326. P(at least one prize) = 1 − (49/50)^100 ≈ 0.867.
